motivation_thread:
CORE_NARRATIVE: Traditional light field depth estimation is fundamentally bottlenecked by the **global Lambertian assumption**, causing severe failures in real-world scenes containing **non-Lambertian and mixed materials**. By introducing a **physical material perception mechanism** via **angular frequency analysis** and multi-view geometric consistency, our approach breaks this assumption to achieve **component-aware depth estimation** with high physical interpretability.

INTRO_HOOK: Open with a striking physical example demonstrating how the **global Lambertian assumption** catastrophically fails when encountering **specular and scattering materials** in real-world scenes. This immediately establishes the **Lambertian assumption limitation** as the core bottleneck hindering the generalization of light field technology in **complex environments**.

GAP_BRIDGE: Highlight that while existing methods attempt to implicitly suppress **non-Lambertian** artifacts, they fail to explicitly model the underlying optical physics, leaving the **global Lambertian assumption** intact. This gap necessitates our transition to rigorous **physical optical modeling** that explicitly decouples material properties for **high-precision depth estimation**.

METHOD_JUSTIFY: The design of the **dual-mask physical modeling** and **MRI-like angular frequency analysis** directly stems from the need to explicitly classify and decouple **Lambertian, specular, and scattering** components. Furthermore, the **Geometric Consistency Descriptor (GCD)** and adaptive energy adjustment are specifically formulated to resolve feature ambiguity caused by **non-Lambertian materials**, ensuring robust **component-aware depth estimation**.

EXPERIMENT_VALIDATE: Validate the motivation by showcasing massive performance gains on datasets rich in **specular and scattering materials** (e.g., Apple, David), proving the efficacy of breaking the **global Lambertian assumption**. Ablation studies must explicitly quantify how the **physical material perception mechanism** and **GCD** improve physical interpretability and generalization in **mixed material** scenarios.

CONCLUSION_ECHO: Conclude by reiterating that breaking the **global Lambertian assumption** through **physical optical modeling** represents a vital paradigm shift for light field depth estimation. Emphasize that achieving true **material perception** and **component-aware depth estimation** paves the way for deploying light field technology reliably in highly **complex, real-world environments**.

motivation_summary:
**Direction**: Lambertian Assumption Limitation
**Problem**: Traditional light field depth estimation heavily relies on the global Lambertian reflection assumption, which fails in real-world scenarios containing non-Lambertian (specular/scattering) and mixed materials, leading to severe depth estimation errors.
**Significance**: Real-world scenes are rarely purely Lambertian; ignoring the optical properties of non-Lambertian materials limits model generalization and physical interpretability, hi

candidates.problem: Traditional light field depth estimation heavily relies on the global Lambertian reflection assumption, which fails in real-world scenarios containing non-Lambertian (specular/scattering) and mixed materials, leading to severe depth estimation errors.
candidates.significance: Real-world scenes are rarely purely Lambertian; ignoring the optical properties of non-Lambertian materials limits model generalization and physical interpretability, hindering the application of light field technology in complex environments.
candidates.approach: Introduces a physical material perception mechanism based on dual masks and MRI-like angular frequency analysis, utilizing 2D-DFT spectral energy distribution for lightweight material classification and an inverse wavevector parsing framework for component-aware depth estimation.
candidates.key_claim: Breaking the global Lambertian assumption through rigorous physical optical modeling and frequency domain analysis to achieve material perception and high-precision depth estimation in non-Lambertian scenes.

candidates.direction: Feature Ambiguity in Reflections and Textures
candidates.problem: In regions with complex textures and specular reflections, multi-view image features become blurred and confused, reducing the contrast between Lambertian and non-Lambertian regions and causing severe smooth artifacts.
candidates.significance: Feature ambiguity directly degrades the quality of prior features required by depth estimation networks, making it difficult to distinguish true geometric depth changes from photometric variations caused by surface materials, thus drastically reducing depth map accuracy.
candidates.approach: Proposes a Geometric Consistency Descriptor (GCD) to quantify surface physical roughness, combined with a double-subtraction strategy and SAM ternary mask guidance to adaptively suppress and enhance energy maps for different material regions, eliminating smooth artifacts.
candidates.key_claim: Effectively eliminating feature ambiguity and smooth artifacts caused by reflections and complex textures by quantifying multi-view consistency and introducing adaptive mask-guided energy adjustment.

candidates.problem: Traditional Epipolar Plane Image (EPI) architectures typically utilize only a subset of views (e.g., 28 out of 81), suffering from severe angular undersampling and directional rigidity, while struggling to handle the failure of view-consistency assumptions in non-Lambertian regions.
candidates.significance: Low view utilization wastes rich geometric information in light field data, causing model performance to hit a ceiling despite large parameter counts; meanwhile, forcing consistency assumptions on non-Lambertian regions introduces systematic matching errors.
candidates.approach: Designs the MACVO-Net architecture to construct an explicit multi-scale cost volume using all 81 views, leveraging the naturally flat cost volume signals in non-Lambertian regions as explicit uncertainty priors to guide the U-Net multi-scale decoder for depth inference.
candidates.key_claim: Overcoming the performance ceiling of light field depth estimation by abandoning the angular undersampling of EPI architectures and utilizing full-angle explicit cost volumes with uncertainty signals.
